Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg`
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD contains only standard variable assignments and function definitions. The `source` array points to the official GNU FTP server and translationproject.org, both expected upstream locations. All entries have SHA-512 checksums provided, so integrity is verified during download. No command substitutions or dangerous code (curl, eval, base64, etc.) are present at the top level or in a `pkgver()` function. The `prepare()`, `build()`, and `package()` functions are not executed by `makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare`. There is no risk of malicious code execution during source fetching or PKGBUILD sourcing.
</details>
<summary>No malicious code in top-level or source fetching.</summary>
</security_assessment>

+ Initial PKGBUILD audit complete [grey50]-- No malicious code in top-level or source fetching.[/]

audits.body:
Cloning https://aur.archlinux.org/lib32-gdbm.git to /home/mgalgs/aur-sleuth-data/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-wnna_k41...
Cloned repository to /home/mgalgs/aur-sleuth-data/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-wnna_k41
Analyzing lib32-gdbm AUR package (working in /home/mgalgs/aur-sleuth-data/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-wnna_k41) with qwen/qwen3-235b-a22b-2507 from https://openrouter.ai/api/v1
Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg`
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD defines standard sources: the main gdbm source tarball from GNU's official FTP site and multiple translation .po files from the trusted translationproject.org domain. All URLs are over HTTPS (except the FTP one, which is standard for GNU sources and expected for public data). The `sha512sums` array is fully populated with matching entries for every source file, so no checksums are skipped. There is no command substitution, `pkgver()` function, or top-level executable code outside of the standard array declarations. The variables used (`pkgname`, `pkgver`, etc.) are static and benign.

The only dynamic element is the use of `$_translation_version` in the source URLs, but it is safely assigned a static value (`1.26`) matching `pkgver`. There is no evidence of code execution during sourcing, no network operations conditional on PKGBUILD logic, and no data exfiltration. The file operations in `prepare()`, `build()`, and `package()` are standard for a packaging script but are irrelevant here due to `--noprepare` and `--nobuild`. Thus, merely sourcing this PKGBUILD and fetching sources poses no security risk.
</details>
<summary>
All sources are static and checksummed, no dynamic execution.
</summary>
</security_assessment>

+ Initial PKGBUILD audit complete [grey50]-- All sources are static and checksummed, no dynamic execution.[/]
